title: Moroccan Chicken Tagine with Olives and Lemon

subs.name: Bone-in mutton pieces; increase covered braising time to 50 minutes before adding olives
subs.notes: lamb shanks are the most traditional Moroccan substitute

ingredients.name: green olives, pitted
visual_checks: Kalamata olives add a richer flavour
ingredients.notes: Kalamata olives add a richer flavour

subs.name: Kalamata olives for deeper brine, or skip entirely and add 1 tbsp capers for a different salt note
1 tbsp white vinegar plus 0.5 tsp sugar for a similar sweet-sour note if lemons are unavailable

ingredients.name: saffron strands
visual_checks: Use an extra pinch of turmeric for colour if unavailable
ingredients.notes: Use an extra pinch of turmeric for colour if unavailable

subs.name: An extra pinch of turmeric for colour; the flavour difference is noticeable but the dish still works well

steps.text: In a large bowl, toss the chicken pieces with turmeric, cumin, coriander powder, cinnamon, black pepper and half the minced garlic. Mix until the pieces are evenly coated. Let the chicken rest while you prepare the rest — even 10 minutes of marinating improves the flavour.

Heat 2 tbsp oil in a heavy-bottomed pot over medium-high flame. Sear the chicken in batches — do not crowd the pot — for 3–4 minutes per side until the pieces are deep golden with some charred edges. Remove to a plate and set aside.

Reduce the flame to medium and add the remaining oil to the same pot. Add the sliced onions and cook, stirring occasionally, for 6 minutes until soft and translucent. Add the remaining garlic and grated ginger; stir for 1 minute until the raw smell disappears.

If using saffron, dissolve the strands in 3 tbsp of warm water and pour into the pot. Return all the seared chicken pieces and any resting juices to the pot. Pour in 300 ml water so the liquid comes about halfway up the chicken.

Bring to a gentle boil, then reduce to a low simmer, cover tightly and cook for 30 minutes. After 30 minutes, the chicken should be almost tender — pierce the thickest piece with a knife and the juices should run clear.

Add the olives, the juice of both lemons and the zest of one lemon. Simmer uncovered for 8–10 minutes — the sauce will reduce and turn glossy and the chicken will be fall-off-the-bone tender. Taste and adjust salt.
Scatter chopped coriander leaves and parsley over the top. Serve hot directly from the pot with crusty bread, steamed couscous (or rava), or plain basmati rice.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: olives → Kalamata olives for deeper brine, or skip entirely and add 1 tbsp capers for a different salt note; saffron → An extra pinch of turmeric for colour; the flavour difference is noticeable but the dish still works well; chicken → Bone-in mutton pieces; increase covered braising time to 50 minutes before adding olives (lamb shanks are the most traditional Moroccan substitute); lemon → 1 tbsp white vinegar plus 0.5 tsp sugar for a similar sweet-sour note if lemons are unavailable
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Cook in a heavy-bottomed handi or deep kadai with a tight-fitting lid. Saffron (kesar) is widely available at Indian mithai shops and is the same ingredient. Serve over sooji (rava) prepared by the absorption method as a couscous substitute — 1 part rava, 1.25 parts boiling water, covered for 5 minutes then fluffed with a fork.
